content:
6 min read
Just now

--

We are building an AI-powered video editor that runs directly in the browser. Users can import media, edit a multi-track timeline, generate captions and AI voiceovers, process audio, and export a finished video without installing a desktop application.
The early version focused on formats browsers handle well: MP4 with H.264/AAC and WebM with VP8/VP9 and Opus. That kept the implementation fast and predictable — but real users do not always upload browser-friendly files.
They upload MKV, MOV, and other containers. Inside those files we may find H.264, HEVC, AAC, AC3, PCM, or other codecs. A file that works perfectly in VLC, Premiere Pro, or DaVinci Resolve may still fail in a browser video element.
This is how we redesigned the media pipeline without sacrificing the fast path for ordinary MP4 files.
1. Container and codec are different problems
MP4, MKV, MOV, and WebM are containers. H.264, VP9, AAC, AC3, and Opus are codecs.
Two MKV files can behave very differently:
• MKV + H.264 video + AAC audio
• MKV + HEVC video + AC3 audio
The extension alone cannot tell us whether the browser can decode a file. The import pipeline needs to identify the real container, inspect its tracks, read codec information, and check the browser’s runtime capabilities.
Our import flow is now:
File signature detection → media probing → capability detection → pipeline selection
We use the file extension and MIME type as hints, but we also inspect binary signatures. MP4 and MOV are based on the ISO Base Media File Format and usually expose an ftyp box. MKV and WebM are based on EBML and commonly begin with the 1A 45 DF A3 signature.
Signature detection identifies the container. Media probing then tells us the video codec, audio codec, resolution, frame rate, duration, time base, and available tracks.
2. Why not convert everything with FFmpeg.wasm?
The simplest compatibility strategy is to transcode every upload into MP4. It works, but it is expensive inside a browser.
A full FFmpeg.wasm workflow introduces several costs:
• A large WebAssembly runtime
• Extra initialization time
• High CPU usage during full transcoding
• Multiple in-memory copies of the source, intermediate data, and output
• Unnecessary re-encoding when the source video is already H.264
For large files, this can delay the moment when a user is able to start editing. It can also create significant memory pressure on laptops and mobile devices.
We kept FFmpeg.wasm, but changed its role. It is no longer the default entry point. It is the final compatibility fallback.
3. The hybrid media architecture
The current architecture uses three levels:
Browser-native media → LibAV.js + WebCodecs → FFmpeg.wasm fallback
Browser-native path
Standard MP4 and WebM files continue to use the browser’s native media stack. This is the fastest path and has the lowest startup and memory cost.
LibAV.js + WebCodecs path
For containers such as MKV or MOV that cannot be handed directly to a video element, LibAV.js probes and demuxes the tracks. Compatible encoded video packets can then be sent to WebCodecs.
This is especially useful when only part of a file is incompatible. For example:
H.264 video → demux → WebCodecs
AC3 audio → LibAV.js decode → PCM/WAV
Instead of re-encoding an entire video because the audio track is AC3, we preserve the compatible video path and decode only the unsupported audio.
FFmpeg.wasm fallback
If the browser cannot decode the codec, timestamps are malformed, the pixel format is unusual, or the file needs a full compatibility conversion, FFmpeg.wasm remains available as the fallback.
The core rule is simple:
Do not transcode when native playback works. Do not re-encode when demuxing is enough. Use full transcoding only when it is actually necessary.
4. Keeping compatibility code out of the startup path
Most users still import ordinary MP4 files. They should not pay the loading and initialization cost of advanced compatibility runtimes.
LibAV.js and FFmpeg.wasm are loaded lazily only when a file requires them. Media probing, demuxing, and audio decoding run in Web Workers so the editor UI and timeline remain responsive.
We also cache probe results so thumbnails, preview, waveform generation, and export do not repeatedly inspect the same file.
Every long-running media task receives a task ID and supports cancellation. If a user removes a file or closes a project, background processing stops instead of continuing to consume CPU and memory.
5. A unified internal media model
Format support becomes difficult to maintain if the timeline needs separate logic for MP4, MKV, MOV, and WebM.
Our compatibility layer converts every supported input into the same internal media description. The timeline works with normalized properties such as duration, video codec, resolution, frame rate, audio sample rate, channel count, and decoder source.
The timeline, captions, voiceover, thumbnails, waveform rendering, and export systems do not need to know whether the original upload was an MKV or MP4 file.
This separation makes it much easier to add new formats later.
6. Simpler export options
More backend capability does not mean the user interface needs more controls.
At one point, the export dialog exposed container, video codec, audio codec, frame rate, quality tiers, keyframe interval, presets, range settings, and pipeline options. Technically powerful — but unnecessarily complicated for most users.
We simplified the visible format list to four practical combinations:
• MP4 · H.264 + AAC — the default and most compatible option
• MOV · H.264 + AAC — convenient for Final Cut Pro, Premiere Pro, and DaVinci Resolve
• WebM · VP9 + Opus — a strong web-focused compression option
• WebM · VP8 + Opus — useful for older WebM workflows
The advanced controls that remain useful are video bitrate and audio bitrate.
The implementation can be complex. The product experience should stay simple.
7. Fixing the 5-second video that exported as 17 seconds
During this work, we found an important timing bug. The browser preview showed a five-second project, but the exported file lasted seventeen seconds.
The problem was not the encoder or the container. An old duration calculation included an estimated script-reading duration. A seventeen-second voiceover estimate was extending the export even though the real timeline ended at five seconds.
The fix was architectural: export duration must be derived only from real timeline clips and track endpoints — not from script estimates, editor viewport horizons, or stale project metadata.
At 30 fps, a five-second project should produce exactly 150 planned frames. Each frame is rendered from its deterministic timeline timestamp.
8. Deterministic offline rendering
MediaRecorder is useful as a compatibility path, but it depends on real-time playback. Page load, CPU contention, background-tab throttling, and scheduling can affect timing.
Our high-quality export path uses deterministic offline rendering:
Timeline timestamp → shared composition renderer → WebCodecs video encoding → OfflineAudioContext mixing → final MP4, MOV, or WebM container
Every frame is resolved from an exact timestamp. The same composition logic is shared by preview and export for visuals, captions, stickers, masks, keyframes, animations, and transitions.
MediaRecorder remains available when browser capabilities require it, but it is no longer the primary high-quality path.
9. Verifying the file, not just the UI
An “Export complete” message does not prove that the output is correct.
Our browser end-to-end tests export a real file, read it back, and verify:
• Container and codec
• Video and audio tracks
• Dimensions and duration
• Frame count
• Visible captions and transparent sticker composition
• The presence of actual audio data
This catches failures that a UI-only test cannot detect.
What we learned
Supporting MKV and MOV in a browser editor is not about adding more extensions to a file picker. It requires container detection, track probing, codec capability checks, demuxing, selective decoding, timestamp management, memory control, cancellation, deterministic rendering, and output verification.
The final architecture is deliberately layered:
Browser-native first. LibAV.js and WebCodecs when necessary. FFmpeg.wasm as the compatibility fallback.
The result is that ordinary MP4 users keep a fast native experience, while MKV and MOV users can enter the same editing workflow through a more capable media pipeline.
Under the hood, the system is complex. For the user, the experience remains simple: upload media, edit the timeline, choose an output format, and export.
Project

Live demo: https://video-editor.ai-creator.top/
